# Chronic kidney disease

> The cascade reaches its final organ. The same insulin-resistant, high-pressure biology that ran through
> obesity, diabetes and the arteries now scars the kidney — and in Singapore this is overwhelmingly the
> downstream damage of diabetes. Here the diet changes character: instead of *preventing* disease, it must
> stand in for a failing organ, doing some of the work the kidney can no longer do. The task is to
> **recognise the five nutritional levers — protein, sodium, potassium, phosphate and fluid — and refer**,
> never to prescribe the renal diet, which is individualised, specialist work.

## Chronic kidney disease at a glance

This chapter closes the cardiometabolic-renal cascade that the *Obesity & Metabolic Syndrome*, *Type 2
Diabetes* and *Cardiovascular Disease & Hypertension* chapters opened: chronic kidney disease (CKD) is the
shared downstream organ damage of the same drivers. It is common, largely **silent** — detected by blood and
urine tests, not symptoms — and progressive [@kdigo2024ckd]. In Singapore its story is a diabetes story:
about **6 people a day** begin treatment for kidney failure, more than **9,000** are on dialysis, and roughly
**2 in 3** new cases are driven by diabetes [@nkfsKeyStats; @ho2024stemmingckd]. That single fact reframes the
whole chapter.

Three habits of mind run through it:

- **The most powerful "renal diet" is upstream.** Because most CKD is the organ damage of poorly controlled diabetes and hypertension, the highest-leverage nutritional intervention is glycaemic and blood-pressure control years earlier — owned by the *Type 2 Diabetes* and *Cardiovascular Disease & Hypertension* chapters, not this one [@ahaCKM2023; @ho2024stemmingckd].
- **There is no single "renal diet."** It is individualised, dietitian-led medical nutrition therapy that changes with the stage of disease and the dialysis modality — a printout is not a prescription [@kdoqi2020nutrition; @aceCKD2023].
- **Advice that is healthy for the public can reverse in CKD.** "Eat more potassium," a potassium salt substitute, the DASH pattern — each is good general advice that can become *dangerous* once the kidney can no longer clear a potassium load. Recognising where the population message flips is the core skill here.

CKD is defined and staged by cause, by **eGFR** (an estimate of filtration, in categories G1–G5) and by
**albuminuria** (protein leaking into the urine, categories A1–A3), laid out on a colour-coded risk "heat map"
that Singapore's national guidance follows [@kdigo2024ckd; @aceCKD2023]. The goal at this stage is recognition,
not threshold recall: **a low eGFR with heavy albuminuria means high-risk CKD** — a patient whose diet, from
that point on, is a specialist matter.

## The science underneath: why a failing kidney rewrites the diet

Why does kidney disease change what a person may eat, when heart disease mostly changed *how much* of certain
foods? Because the kidney is not only a filter — it is the body's main **regulator of what stays and what
goes**. The healthy kidney clears sodium, potassium, phosphate, acid and water, and it activates vitamin D; the
detailed handling of these — how the body balances sodium and potassium, and water — belongs to the
*Micronutrients, Hydration & Electrolytes* chapter, and here we follow what happens when that handling fails.

![In health (left) the kidney clears sodium, potassium, phosphate, acid and water and activates vitamin D, so the diet need not manage them. As nephrons are lost in CKD (right), these accumulate in the blood — raising blood pressure and fluid load, risking hyperkalaemia, driving bone–mineral disease and acidosis — so five dietary levers (sodium, potassium, phosphate, fluid and protein) must now do some of the work the kidney no longer can. All of it is delivered as individualised, dietitian-led therapy.](/figures/mechanisms/ckd-diet-levers.svg){#fig-ckd-levers width="92%" fig-align="center"}

As functioning **nephrons** — the kidney's filtering units — are progressively lost, the substances they used
to clear begin to **accumulate** [@kdigo2024ckd]. Retained sodium and water raise blood volume and blood
pressure, and cause oedema. Potassium can no longer be excreted freely, so a dietary load that a healthy
kidney would shed can push blood potassium to a dangerous level — **hyperkalaemia**, which disturbs the heart's
rhythm [@hunter2019hyperkalemia]. Phosphate is retained, and the failing kidney also stops activating vitamin
D, together driving the bone-and-vessel disorder known as **CKD–mineral and bone disease** [@kdigo2017ckdmbd].
And the metabolic waste of protein — acid and urea — builds up too. The diet must now help manage each of
these: that is why the levers exist. But there is a counter-force. Uraemia blunts appetite while illness
raises the body's demands, so patients readily slide into **protein-energy wasting** — undernutrition layered
on top of a kidney disease — which makes reflexive restriction genuinely hazardous [@fouque2008pew;
@carrero2013pewetiology]. The whole art of the renal diet is holding these two dangers in balance, which is
exactly why it is specialist work.

## There is no single "renal diet"

The first thing to unlearn is the idea of *the* kidney diet — a fixed list to hand out. Renal nutrition is
**individualised medical nutrition therapy**: it depends on the stage of disease, on whether the patient is on
dialysis and which kind, on their potassium and phosphate blood levels, and on their nutritional state — and
international guidelines specify it should be delivered by a **renal dietitian** [@kdoqi2020nutrition;
@aceCKD2023]. The same food can be encouraged in one patient and limited in another. What a generalist does is
**recognise the five levers, reinforce the one that is safely reinforceable (sodium), avoid the do-no-harm
traps, and refer** — which is what the rest of the chapter builds toward.

## Protein: the lever that flips at dialysis

Protein is the most misunderstood lever, because the advice **reverses at dialysis**. Before dialysis, protein
is modestly *restricted* to reduce the waste load on failing kidneys; once a patient starts dialysis — which
is itself catabolic and removes protein — the target is *raised* again [@kdoqi2020nutrition;
@lambert2022kdoqicommentary]. Same patient, opposite advice, depending on the stage. That alone is a reason to
recognise the lever and refer rather than apply a number from memory.

The benefit of restriction is also **genuinely debated**, and it makes a good lesson in reading a landmark
trial. The famous MDRD study found only a small, statistically fragile effect on kidney-function decline in its
**primary** analysis; the confident "protein restriction slows CKD" reading came from a later **secondary**
analysis [@klahr1994mdrd; @levey1999mdrd]. So the fair reading is benefit *and* caveat — not a settled
prescription.

::: {.callout-warning}
## The other danger is too little, not too much
The reflex "low protein is good for kidneys" can do real harm. Push protein down without safeguarding
calories and monitoring, and patients — especially older ones — tip into **protein-energy wasting**: a defined
syndrome of low body and muscle mass, poor intake and abnormal blood chemistry that is common on dialysis
(roughly a third to a half of patients) and strongly linked to death [@fouque2008pew; @carrero2018pewprev;
@bonanni2011pewmortality]. Adequate **energy** is what makes any protein target safe — without enough
calories, the body simply breaks down its own muscle for fuel [@kdoqi2020nutrition]. So protein targets are
modest, stage-specific and dietitian-set, and unsupervised low-protein advice is exactly the wrong instinct
[@piccoli2023lowprotein]. This under-tail links forward to the *Undernutrition & Malnutrition* chapter.
:::

## Sodium: the lever you *can* reinforce

Sodium is the one lever a generalist can legitimately reinforce, because the advice does **not** reverse in
CKD — cutting salt helps here for the same reasons it helps in hypertension, plus it reduces protein leak and
lets blood-pressure drugs work better [@kdigo2024ckd; @kdoqi2020nutrition]. Guidance suggests keeping sodium
under about **2 g a day (roughly 5 g of salt)**, though the evidence is of low certainty and rests on surrogate
measures like blood pressure [@kdigo2024ckd]. Singaporeans start badly placed: about **9 in 10 exceed the
limit**, averaging **~3,600 mg a day**, most of it hidden in soups, sauces and eaten-out food [@hpb2023sodium].
The population sodium-and-blood-pressure story is owned by the *Cardiovascular Disease & Hypertension* chapter;
what CKD adds is a hard caution on *how* people are often told to cut salt — which the next section addresses.

## Potassium: when "eat more" reverses

Here is the chapter's signature reversal. For the general public, **potassium is protective** — the WHO
recommends eating *more* of it (fruit, vegetables, legumes) to lower blood pressure — but that same guideline
**explicitly excludes people whose kidneys cannot excrete potassium** [@who2012potassium]. In advanced CKD, a
potassium load the healthy kidney would clear can instead accumulate to a dangerous level and disturb the heart
rhythm [@hunter2019hyperkalemia]. The field is moving away from blanket fruit-and-vegetable bans toward
**individualised, blood-level-guided** advice — whole-food potassium is handled differently by the body than
the number on a label suggests, and over-restriction worsens diet quality — but that individualising is a
dietitian's job [@kdoqi2020nutrition; @clegg2020potassiumplant].

Two potassium hazards deserve to be recognised on sight, because they carry more risk than an ordinary
high-potassium food:

<div data-island="renal-flip" data-src="/diagrams/renal-flip.yml"></div>

::: {.callout-warning}
## Two potassium traps: the salt substitute and the star fruit
**The potassium salt substitute.** "Low-sodium" or "heart-healthy" salt swaps sodium chloride for *potassium*
chloride. For most people that is a genuine cardiovascular lever — in a large trial it cut strokes and deaths —
but that trial **deliberately excluded people with serious kidney disease and those on potassium-raising drugs**
(ACE inhibitors, ARBs, potassium-sparing diuretics), so its safety data do *not* cover CKD, where the swap can
precipitate life-threatening hyperkalaemia [@neal2021ssass; @xu2024saltsubstitutes; @doorenbos2003saltsubstitute].
This is the do-no-harm caveat the *Cardiovascular Disease & Hypertension* chapter signposted to this one — and
here it lands.

**Star fruit and belimbing (bilimbi).** These are not merely high in potassium: they carry a **neurotoxin**
that healthy kidneys clear but failing ones cannot, causing confusion, seizures and death
in CKD — with mortality reported in a third of intoxicated patients with kidney failure [@chua2017starfruit;
@caetano2017bilimbi]. For a CKD patient these are an **absolute avoid**, not a portion-controlled food — a
locally important warning, since both are common in Singapore gardens and juices [@nkfsEatingRightCKD].
:::

## Phosphate: the source matters more than the number

Retained phosphate is a quiet driver of CKD–mineral and bone disease, and the teaching point is that **the form
matters more than the milligrams** [@kdigo2017ckdmbd; @gonzalezparra2012phosphorus]. Phosphate that occurs
naturally in whole foods (meat, dairy, legumes) is only partly absorbed — roughly **40–60%**, and less from
plant sources — whereas the **inorganic phosphate additives** used to preserve and enhance processed foods are
absorbed almost completely, up to **~90–100%** [@noori2010phosphorus; @gonzalezparra2012phosphorus].

![Not all dietary phosphate is absorbed equally. Phosphate bound in plant foods (phytate) is the least available; the phosphate naturally in animal foods is partly absorbed; but the inorganic phosphate *additives* in processed foods are almost completely absorbed — and are often invisible on the label. Preferring fresh over processed, and scanning ingredient lists for "-PHOS" additives, is the real lever.](/figures/mechanisms/phosphate-absorption.svg){#fig-phosphate width="80%" fig-align="center"}

The catch is that these additives are often **not on the label**: "enhanced" meats and many processed products
carry a large hidden phosphate load, so reading the printed phosphorus number badly under-counts what is
actually absorbed [@sherman2009enhancedmeat; @stjules2017phosphateadditive]. The practical lever is to prefer
**fresh over processed** and to scan ingredient lists for additives ending in "**-PHOS**"
[@bump2016organicphosphorus; @gutierrez2013phosphateadditives]. Whether phosphate then needs a **binder** — a
drug taken with meals — is a specialist decision, and the bone side of CKD–mineral and bone disease (calcium,
vitamin D, PTH, fracture risk) belongs to the *Bone & Joint Health* chapter [@tsuchiya2021phosphate;
@ketteler2025ckdmbd].

::: {.callout-tip}
## 🍜 Eating out
The renal-diet traps in Singapore's food environment are specific and local. High-potassium: **coconut
milk/kaya, durian, bananas, kangkong, dhal, essence of chicken, stock cubes, Milo/Horlicks-type malt drinks,
and strong kopi or teh**. High-additive-phosphate: **luncheon and canned meat, sausages, meat patties,
processed cheese, malt drinks and colas**. Dietitians teach a **leaching** trick for vegetables (soak cut
pieces, then boil and discard the water) to lower potassium — but the *amounts* are individualised, so the
generalist's job is to **flag these and refer**, not to hand out quantities [@nkfsEatingRightCKD].
:::

## Fluid: individualised and specialist

Fluid is the lever generalists most often over-apply. Early CKD usually needs **no** fluid restriction; limits
matter mainly in advanced disease and on dialysis, where retained water shows up as weight gain between
sessions, ankle and lung oedema, and breathlessness [@kdoqi2020nutrition]. There is no universal number — a
fluid target is individualised and set by the renal team. The generalist's job is to **recognise the signs of
overload, tie thirst to salt intake, and refer**, not to prescribe a millilitre limit.

## When to refer

Once CKD is confirmed, individualised nutrition *is* the treatment, and it is dietitian-led. Refer when eGFR is
falling or in the lower stages (roughly G3b and below), when CKD coexists with diabetes, when potassium or
phosphate blood levels are abnormal, when there is weight loss or any sign of protein-energy wasting, and at
the start of dialysis — in short, whenever the advice needed exceeds general healthy eating [@kdoqi2020nutrition;
@aceCKD2023]. In Singapore the National Kidney Foundation provides most subsidised dialysis and runs the renal
patient-education that a referral plugs into [@mohNkfDialysis2026; @nkfsEatingRightCKD]. The wider referral map
— who does what across the multidisciplinary team — is the subject of later teaching on interprofessional
practice and referral.

## The Singapore picture

**The burden is a diabetes story.** Singapore has one of the highest rates of diabetes-driven kidney failure
in the world: about **6 people a day** start treatment for kidney failure, over **9,000** are on dialysis, and
roughly **2 in 3** new cases are due to diabetes [@nkfsKeyStats; @leo2020dialysissg]. Local CKD prevalence
tracks the cardiometabolic drivers closely — far higher in people with diabetes or hypertension than without —
and is projected to rise toward **1 in 4 residents** as those conditions climb [@ho2024stemmingckd;
@wong2018ckdprojection]. This is the population counterpart to the individual renal diet: the biggest kidney
win is made years upstream.

**The policy frame joins the dots.** Because diabetic kidney disease dominates new dialysis, the national **War
on Diabetes** is simultaneously the country's most important CKD-prevention strategy [@mohWarOnDiabetes], and
the international **Cardiovascular-Kidney-Metabolic** framing is the scaffold that unifies this chapter with the
three before it [@ahaCKM2023]. National **ACE guidance** on CKD aligns to the same heat-map staging and is
pragmatic about referral — not every patient needs a dietitian, but those with complex needs do [@aceCKD2023].

**The flavour clash is real, and it drives poor intake.** A low-salt, potassium-aware, low-additive-phosphate
diet runs against a flavour-dense hawker palate built on soy, sambal, coconut and preserved dishes — a genuine
reason patients struggle to eat well and slide toward wasting, and another reason the plan needs a dietitian
who can adapt it to how people actually eat [@carrero2013pewetiology].

::: {.callout-important}
## You recognise the levers — you don't prescribe the renal diet
There is no renal-diet prescription to memorise here, and that is the point. The five levers — protein, sodium,
potassium, phosphate, fluid — are recognised, but the *quantities* are individualised to the patient's stage,
dialysis status and blood results by a renal dietitian [@kdoqi2020nutrition; @aceCKD2023]. The generalist's job
is to reinforce the one safely reinforceable lever (**sodium**), avoid the **do-no-harm traps** (a potassium
salt substitute or star fruit in the wrong patient), watch for **wasting**, and **refer** — which is exactly
the altitude this chapter is pitched at.
:::

## So — what does a "kidney diet" actually look like?

It looks like a *dietitian*, not a list. A person with early CKD mostly needs their diabetes and blood pressure
controlled and their salt cut — the ordinary heart-healthy plate, done well. As the disease advances, the diet
becomes a careful, individualised balance: enough protein and energy to avoid wasting but not so much as to
overload failing kidneys; potassium and phosphate managed to blood levels rather than banned; fluid limited
only when overload appears — all of it tuned to the person and their stage. The generalist recognises the five
levers, reinforces sodium, sidesteps the potassium traps, watches for wasting, and refers. With that, the
cardiometabolic-renal cascade is complete — and the book turns from the diseases of *too much* to the other
tail of malnutrition: *too little*.

## Check your understanding

<div data-island="quiz" data-src="/quizzes/chronic-kidney-disease.quiz.yml"></div>

## Practise: Uncle Lim and the "eat more banana" advice

Uncle Lim is 62, has had type 2 diabetes for twelve years, and his kidney function has slipped — his eGFR is
38 with heavy protein in the urine, and he is on an ACE inhibitor. He arrives with a printout from a well-meaning
friend: *"Doctor, it says I should eat more banana for my heart, and switch to this low-sodium salt — good or
not?"* Later he mentions he has started skipping meals because he read that "low protein saves the kidneys,"
and asks what single change would help most. Work through the visit: classify his risk, handle the potassium
and salt-substitute trap safely, catch the protein mistake, pick the one lever you can reinforce — and notice
where prescribing a number, rather than referring, would be the wrong reflex. Your choices branch the case.

<div data-island="case" data-src="/cases/mr-lim-ckd.case.yml"></div>
